learning_outcomes.description: Understand and assess police roles and functions and styles of policing.
Understand the pressures and demands placed on the operations of modern police agencies.
Identify factors that impact on police legitimacy and police effectiveness.
Critically evaluate how to improve the operations of modern police agencies.
Critically examine and evaluate current issues in policing.

assessments.task_description:
Discussion Board 1 begins: Week 4 Monday 17/8/25, 8.00am - ends Friday 21/8/25, 5.00pm.

Discussion Board 2 begins: Week 9 Monday 21/9/25, 8.00am - ends Friday 25/9/25, 5.00pm.

Students are required to participate in two online discussion forums via Blackboard, focusing on specific issues raised during the course and in the reading material. Students may initiate discussions, ask questions, answer them, or make comments about issues reported in the media relevant to the topic of policing. The course coordinator will help to initiate discussion and post questions in Blackboard to help generate dialogue between students.

Students must participate by logging into the CRIM2200 Blackboard site and access the Discussion Board under the Assessment content area, which will link them to the relevant discussion forum for each session. Each forum will last 5 days (see assessment schedule for start and end times and days).

You will be graded on your participation, and it is expected that you actively contribute to the forums by engaging other students, rather than just submitting one answer in response to the question the course coordinator has posted. Each forum will be worth 15%. You will be graded on your overall participation in each session and how often you participate. You will also be assessed on the quality of your contributions, including evidence of preparation and prior reading of the course reading material. Please do not leave it to the last minute to contribute. Those who make an effort to participate throughout the five days will get a better mark than those who make contributions on a single day. The aim is “participation” – to engage with your peers. Discussion is a key learning activity. A number of well-thought-out comments or questions/answers submitted throughout the 5 days will be well regarded. Students will be notified on the initiation of each discussion forum. Make sure you monitor Blackboard announcements. For guidance, the following criteria has been set to help students understand how they will be assessed:

To pass students need to do the following:

  1. Show evidence of having done some preparation prior to the discussion. This might be shown by referring to the lecture content or reading material and by demonstrating some understanding of concepts raised in the discussion.
  2. Participate on a regular basis throughout the five days.
  3. Be willing to engage with other students. This could be shown by responding to other student postings.
  4. Show evidence of linking concepts and theories to real life situations and case studies. This might be shown by making some comment about an example covered in the media and reflecting on how it relates to course material or the discussion forum topic.
  5. Provide examples to ground the issue being discussed – e.g. outlining some research evidence that supports your opinion.
  6. Makes use of additional external material such websites or journal articles.
  7. Ask questions and offer your viewpoint on a topic and back this up with evidence.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The online discussion forums can be accessed via the course Blackboard site, under Assessments.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The In-class quiz will take place in the scheduled class time in week 11. The quiz will be based on content up to and including week 9. It will comprise multiple choice and short answer items that will assess your comprehension of the readings and class material. The quiz is closed book and is to be done individually. It will assess your knowledge of material covered in lectures and required readings. Students are strongly advised to keep up with the required readings before each class in order to perform well.

Timing: Students needing additional time for the quiz will be permitted 90 minutes to complete it.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Task Description: During class each week, students will be required to reflect on course readings and class activities/group discussions. At the end of each seminar, students will submit their worksheet summarising the results of their group discussions. The worksheets must be submitted at the end of class.

Preparation: Students are required to prepare by reading the week’s assigned readings in advance of the class. Worksheets will be distributed to students in class each week.

When: Students will be required to submit worksheets in a minimum of eight (8) classes. However, it is important to note that attendance and participation is expected in all classes. Each student's best 8 worksheets will count towards the 40% mark for this assessment.

NOTE: It is important that all students actively participate in these group exercises. Since there are twelve (12) possible worksheets to complete and eight (8) count towards the final grade, there will be no alternative to this assessment, deferrals or extensions. If a student misses a seminar they will forfeit the mark for the worksheet.

To pass, students need to do the following:

  1. Show a strong grasp of the key concepts discussed each week.
  2. Present new ideas or critique existing ones related to the topic.
  3. Reflect on the literature related to the topic.
  4. Collaborate and work as a group in completing your worksheet.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: To be submitted in class at the end of each seminar.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Course Introduction

Course overview, history of police and policing, police roles and structures. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Course Introduction Course overview, history of police and policing, police roles and structures.

learning_activities.topic:
Policing Models I

Introduction to Models of Policing: The Standard Model of Policing; Plural Policing. 


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Policing Models I Introduction to Models of Policing: The Standard Model of Policing; Plural Policing.

learning_activities.topic:
Policing Models II

Community policing; Problem-oriented policing; Intelligence led policing; Third party policing. 

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Policing Models II Community policing; Problem-oriented policing; Intelligence led policing; Third party policing.

learning_activities.topic:
Police Legitimacy and Effectiveness

Importance of legitimacy; police effectiveness.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Police Legitimacy and Effectiveness Importance of legitimacy; police effectiveness.

learning_activities.topic:
Police Leadership, Powers and Behaviour

Police leadership; powers and duties; discretion; factors influencing police behaviour (including police culture; police use of force, technology). 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Police Leadership, Powers and Behaviour Police leadership; powers and duties; discretion; factors influencing police behaviour (including police culture; police use of force, technology).

learning_activities.topic:
Oversight, Integrity and Ethics

Inquiries into police corruption; accountability; oversight bodies. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Oversight, Integrity and Ethics Inquiries into police corruption; accountability; oversight bodies.

learning_activities.topic:
Police Investigations

Process of police investigations.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Police Investigations Process of police investigations.

learning_activities.topic:
Policing in Diverse Communities

Representation of diverse communities in the police; police and minority groups; engaging diverse communities.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Policing in Diverse Communities Representation of diverse communities in the police; police and minority groups; engaging diverse communities.

learning_activities.topic:
Police Operations in Context I

Case studies of police work with victims of crime and in policing domestic, family and sexual violence (DFSV). 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Police Operations in Context I Case studies of police work with victims of crime and in policing domestic, family and sexual violence (DFSV).

learning_activities.topic:
Police Operations in Context II

Case studies of specialist policing (e.g., border security, cyber policing, and policing illicit drugs and terrorism). 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Police Operations in Context II Case studies of specialist policing (e.g., border security, cyber policing, and policing illicit drugs and terrorism).

learning_activities.topic:
Representations of Police

Global perceptions and representations of police.  

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Representations of Police Global perceptions and representations of police.

learning_activities.topic:
The Future of Police Practice and Policy

Innovations in police; innovations in crime and changing communities.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: The Future of Police Practice and Policy
learning_activities.topic_description: The Future of Police Practice and Policy Innovations in police; innovations in crime and changing communities.
